title: Smoking Harvard
description: To call How High a guilty pleasure explicitly and wrongly conveys that there should be any amount of shame assigned to the consumption of the film, the wider subgenre, or its ephemera and paraphernalia off-screen.
hook: A stoner comedy about Harvard gets serious: why we shouldn't be embarrassed by the films that make us laugh the hardest.

title: A Particular Beauty: Irmin Schmidt Interviewed
description:
From a childhood obsessed with art to the electroacoustic, treated piano work of new album Requiem via Can's "school of pain", the German composer reflects on a life well lived. Words: Adelle Stripe. Portraits: Robin Maddock

As a founding member of Can, and a composer with an illustrious soundtrack career, Irmin
hook: Can's Irmin Schmidt on electroacoustic pianos, childhood art obsession, and the mentors who taught him to hear strangeness as beauty.

title: 9 Little Odysseys That Don’t Go Very Far, and That’s the Whole Point
description:
I love a rousing epic, but I’m equally drawn to smaller, more interior odysseys—stories set in kitchens, in unassuming towns, or in the mind itself. Unlike larger-than-life quest narratives with a traditional (and traditionally male) protagonist, these little odysseys take place in spaces often coded as female and just as often dismissed as unimportant. But […]

The post 9 L
hook: Forget epic quests—the best odysseys happen in kitchens and minds, where interior lives matter more than grand destinations.

title: Living without my self
description:
Our culture valorises the big, coherent self: reading Robert Musil helps me embrace the beauty of my no-self existence

- by Mette Leonard Høeg
hook: On dissolving the self entirely: what Musil discovered when he stopped pretending to be a coherent person.

title: ‘The Boys’ Review: A Bloody, Brutal Final Season Goes Where Marvel Won’t
description: While Marvel blockbusters bend over backward to keep characters around forever, Eric Kripke's black-comic action series takes a hard look at the consequences of chasing childlike fantasies into adulthood.
hook: The Boys ends where Marvel refuses to go: what happens when you actually let consequences catch up with arrested development.
